I need you to help me build Printonet Multi-Tenant platform. The Printonet Multi-Tenant platform is a multi-tenant eCommerce platform that lets print shop owners instantly launch and manage their own online merch stores, powered by a centralized WooCommerce setup running on a **Hostinger VPS. The system uses a WordPress Multisite network as the core infrastructure, where each tenant store is created as a subdomain and inherits a shared, Git-controlled Printonet storefront theme (based on Storefront theme from WooCommerce) along with a core MU plugin that handles branding, supplier integrations, and business logic. 

Store owners customize their branding and products through a separate Printonet dashboard, while WooCommerce manages products, cart, checkout, and orders. A React-based product customizer is embedded into product pages, allowing customers to design items and generate print-ready files tied to each order. All storefronts are centrally deployed and updated via Git, enabling Printonet to push changes across every store on the VPS instantly, similar to a modern React application workflow.

The main Printonet platform is already built where the users creates an account and integrates their existing suppliers, storefronts (WooCommerce or Shopify), and or can create a brand new store with one click which sends a call to this new platfrom you will help me to build. The WordPress plugin to receive the new store data such as logo, store colors, store name, etc., is already created and will need to be integrated into this new multi-tenant platform.

Very importatnt: what we're building is a type of shopyfy type of platform where print shop owners can go and create a new store really quick. This will be done from the main Printonet platform.

Final Product Vision

Printonet should feel like:

Shopify for print shops
+ WooCommerce engine
+ React product customizer
+ Supplier catalog sync
+ centrally managed storefronts

The goal is to let a print shop launch a custom merch store quickly without managing WordPress, plugins, hosting, or technical setup.

I will connect to the Hostinger VPS via SSH and let you start building
</user_query>

To begin building this properly on your Hostinger VPS, I need one of these two setups:

- **Option A (recommended):** You SSH into the VPS and clone/create the target repo there, then tell me the project path (for example `/var/www/printonet-mt`).
- **Option B:** Give me a local repo path in this workspace and I’ll scaffold everything first, then you deploy it to VPS.

Once you’re ready, I’ll start with this build order (end-to-end, production-oriented):

1. **Infrastructure foundation**
   - WordPress Multisite (subdomain mode) + Nginx + MariaDB + PHP-FPM
   - wildcard DNS/SSL and tenant routing strategy

2. **Repo structure (Git-first)**
   - `wp-content/themes/printonet-storefront` (shared tenant theme)
   - `wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core` (shared tenant business logic)
   - deployment scripts for centralized updates to all stores

3. **Tenant provisioning pipeline**
   - secure API endpoint in MU plugin to receive “create store” payload from main Printonet
   - create site/subdomain + apply branding (logo/colors/name) + activate baseline Woo settings

4. **WooCommerce tenant defaults**
   - global plugin policy, per-tenant options, checkout/cart defaults, tax/shipping placeholders

5. **React product customizer integration**
   - embed flow on product pages
   - save design metadata + print files into order line items

6. **Supplier integration framework**
   - adapter architecture in MU plugin for catalog sync + stock/price updates

7. **Ops and safety**
   - role/capability restrictions for tenants
   - audit logs, backup strategy, staged rollout, and rollback strategy

If you share the VPS project directory after SSH, I’ll immediately start scaffolding the actual code and config files.

content.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: /root/printonet-multitenant/scripts/bootstrap_vps.sh
+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+set -euo pipefail
+
+ENV_FILE="${1:-.env}"
+if [[ ! -f "$ENV_FILE" ]]; then
+  echo "Missing env file: $ENV_FILE"
+  exit 1
+fi
+
+set -a
+source "$ENV_FILE"
+set +a
+
+export DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive
+
+apt-get update
+apt-get install -y nginx mariadb-server redis-server unzip curl jq software-properties-common
+
+add-apt-repository -y ppa:ondrej/php
+apt-get update
+apt-get install -y php8.2-fpm php8.2-cli php8.2-curl php8.2-mbstring php8.2-xml php8.2-mysql php8.2-zip php8.2-gd php8.2-intl php8.2-bcmath php8.2-soap php8.2-imagick
+
+curl -fsSL https://getcomposer.org/installer | php
+mv composer.phar /usr/local/bin/composer
+
+curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/wp-cli/builds/gh-pages/phar/wp-cli.phar -o /usr/local/bin/wp
+chmod +x /usr/local/bin/wp
+
+mysql -uroot <<SQL
+ALTER USER 'root'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY '${DB_ROOT_PASSWORD}';
+CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS ${DB_NAME} CHARACTER SET utf8mb4 COLLATE utf8mb4_unicode_ci;
+CREATE USER IF NOT EXISTS '${DB_USER}'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY '${DB_PASSWORD}';
+GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON ${DB_NAME}.* TO '${DB_USER}'@'localhost';
+FLUSH PRIVILEGES;
+SQL
+
+mkdir -p "${WP_PATH}"
+chown -R www-data:www-data "${WP_PATH}"
+
+cat >/etc/nginx/sites-available/printonet.conf <<NGINX
+server {
+    listen 80;
+    server_name ${APP_DOMAIN} *.${APP_DOMAIN};
+    root ${WP_PATH};
+    index index.php index.html;
+
+    location / {
+        try_files \$uri \$uri/ /index.php?\$args;
+    }
+
+    location ~ \.php$ {
+        include snippets/fastcgi-php.conf;
+        fastcgi_pass unix:/run/php/php8.2-fpm.sock;
+    }
+
+    client_max_body_size 64M;
+}
+NGINX
+
+ln -sf /etc/nginx/sites-available/printonet.conf /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/printonet.conf
+rm -f /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/default
+
+systemctl enable nginx mariadb redis-server php8.2-fpm
+systemctl restart nginx mariadb redis-server php8.2-fpm
+
+echo "Base VPS bootstrap complete."
*** End Patch

content.name: ApplyPatch
content.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: /root/printonet-multitenant/scripts/install_wp_multisite.sh
+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+set -euo pipefail
+
+ENV_FILE="${1:-.env}"
+if [[ ! -f "$ENV_FILE" ]]; then
+  echo "Missing env file: $ENV_FILE"
+  exit 1
+fi
+
+set -a
+source "$ENV_FILE"
+set +a
+
+mkdir -p "$WP_PATH"
+cd "$WP_PATH"
+
+if [[ ! -f "wp-config.php" ]]; then
+  sudo -u www-data wp core download --allow-root
+  sudo -u www-data wp config create \
+    --dbname="$DB_NAME" \
+    --dbuser="$DB_USER" \
+    --dbpass="$DB_PASSWORD" \
+    --dbhost="localhost" \
+    --dbprefix="wp_" \
+    --allow-root
+
+  sudo -u www-data wp core install \
+    --url="$WP_URL" \
+    --title="Printonet Network" \
+    --admin_user="$WP_ADMIN_USER" \
+    --admin_password="$WP_ADMIN_PASSWORD" \
+    --admin_email="$WP_ADMIN_EMAIL" \
+    --skip-email \
+    --allow-root
+fi
+
+sudo -u www-data wp core multisite-convert --subdomains --allow-root
+sudo -u www-data wp plugin install woocommerce --activate-network --allow-root
+
+# Deploy shared code from repo
+REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/.." && pwd)"
+rsync -a --delete "${REPO_ROOT}/wp-content/mu-plugins/" "${WP_PATH}/wp-content/mu-plugins/"
+rsync -a --delete "${REPO_ROOT}/wp-content/themes/printonet-storefront/" "${WP_PATH}/wp-content/themes/printonet-storefront/"
+
+sudo -u www-data wp site list --fields=blog_id,url --allow-root
+
+echo "Multisite install complete."
+echo "Remember to configure wildcard DNS and SSL for *.${APP_DOMAIN}"
*** End Patch

+

+
+```json
+{
+  "success": true,
+  "site_id": 12,
+  "store_url": "https://acme-print.stores.printonet.local"
+}
+```
+
+## Error responses
+
+- `401/403`: invalid API key
+- `409`: tenant already exists
+- `422`: missing required fields
+- `500`: site creation failure
*** End Patch

Created the initial Printonet multi-tenant foundation on your fresh VPS at `/root/printonet-multitenant` so you can start provisioning tenant stores from your main platform.

### What I set up

- **Project scaffold** with docs and install flow in `README.md`
- **Server bootstrap script** in `scripts/bootstrap_vps.sh`
  - Installs Nginx, MariaDB, Redis, PHP 8.2, WP-CLI
  - Creates DB/user
  - Creates Nginx vhost for apex + wildcard subdomains
- **WordPress multisite installer** in `scripts/install_wp_multisite.sh`
  - Installs WP + converts to multisite (subdomain mode)
  - Installs WooCommerce network-wide
  - Syncs shared theme and MU plugin into live `wp-content`
  - Injects provisioning key into `wp-config.php`
- **Core MU plugin** in `wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core`
  - REST endpoint: `POST /wp-json/printonet/v1/provision-store`
  - API-key auth via `X-Printonet-Api-Key`
  - Creates tenant site/subdomain and stores branding options
  - Activates shared theme for new tenant
- **Shared theme scaffold** in `wp-content/themes/printonet-storefront`
  - Child theme over Woo Storefront
  - Injects tenant brand colors from options
- **API contract doc** in `docs/API_CONTRACT.md`
- **Environment template** in `.env.example`

### Important note

- PHP CLI isn’t installed yet in the current shell context, so I couldn’t run `php -l` syntax checks locally before bootstrap.  
  (This is expected on a fresh VPS and gets resolved when bootstrap runs.)

### Run this now

From `/root/printonet-multitenant`:

```bash
cp .env.example .env
# edit .env with your real domain/passwords/keys
sudo bash scripts/bootstrap_vps.sh .env
sudo bash scripts/install_wp_multisite.sh .env
```

### Next step I can do for you immediately

If you want, I’ll continue now with:
- production TLS/wildcard cert setup (`certbot` + wildcard DNS instructions),
- network hardening (rate limit + basic WAF rules),
- and a v2 provisioning flow that also auto-creates tenant admin user + default Woo settings + supplier placeholders.

*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: /root/printonet-multitenant/scripts/setup_tls.sh
+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+set -euo pipefail
+
+ENV_FILE="${1:-.env}"
+if [[ ! -f "$ENV_FILE" ]]; then
+  echo "Missing env file: $ENV_FILE"
+  exit 1
+fi
+
+set -a
+source "$ENV_FILE"
+set +a
+
+if [[ -z "${LETSENCRYPT_EMAIL:-}" ]]; then
+  echo "LETSENCRYPT_EMAIL must be set in ${ENV_FILE}"
+  exit 1
+fi
+
+echo "Requesting certificates for ${APP_DOMAIN} and *.${APP_DOMAIN}"
+echo "Ensure DNS A record for ${APP_DOMAIN} and wildcard subdomain already points to this VPS."
+
+certbot --nginx \
+  --agree-tos \
+  --email "${LETSENCRYPT_EMAIL}" \
+  -d "${APP_DOMAIN}" \
+  -d "*.${APP_DOMAIN}" \
+  --non-interactive || {
+    echo "Wildcard via HTTP challenge may fail on some setups."
+    echo "Use DNS challenge manually if needed:"
+    echo "certbot certonly --manual --preferred-challenges dns -d ${APP_DOMAIN} -d *.${APP_DOMAIN}"
+    exit 1
+  }
+
+systemctl reload nginx
+echo "TLS setup complete."
*** End Patch

Implemented both items.

### Added signed request verification (main platform -> tenant engine)

- New auth helper: `wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core/includes/class-printonet-request-auth.php`

Yes — that’s exactly what it should ask for.

Use these values:

- `PRINTONET_PLATFORM_HMAC_SECRET`
  - This must be the **same exact value** as `PLATFORM_HMAC_SECRET` in your tenant engine `.env` (which gets written as `PRINTONET_PLATFORM_HMAC_SECRET` in `wp-config.php`).
  - Generate a long random string (at least 32+ chars).
  - Example generation:
    - `openssl rand -hex 32`

- `PRINTONET_TENANT_BASE_URL`
  - Your tenant engine base domain, e.g.:
  - `https://stores.yourdomain.com`

*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: /root/printonet-multitenant/scripts/signed_smoke_test.sh
+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+set -euo pipefail
+
+ENV_FILE="${1:-.env}"
+if [[ ! -f "$ENV_FILE" ]]; then
+  echo "Missing env file: $ENV_FILE"
+  exit 1
+fi
+
+set -a
+source "$ENV_FILE"
+set +a
+
+if [[ -z "${WP_URL:-}" || -z "${PLATFORM_HMAC_SECRET:-}" ]]; then
+  echo "WP_URL and PLATFORM_HMAC_SECRET must be set in ${ENV_FILE}"
+  exit 1
+fi
+
+REQUEST_ID="smoke-$(date +%s)"
+TENANT_SLUG="smoke-$(date +%s)"
+BODY="$(cat <<JSON
+{
+  "request_id": "${REQUEST_ID}",
+  "tenant_slug": "${TENANT_SLUG}",
+  "store_name": "Smoke Test Store",
+  "admin_email": "smoke@example.com",
+  "primary_color": "#111111",
+  "secondary_color": "#ffffff"
+}
+JSON
+)"
+
+TIMESTAMP="$(date +%s)"
+SIGNATURE="$(printf "%s.%s" "$TIMESTAMP" "$BODY" | openssl dgst -sha256 -hmac "$PLATFORM_HMAC_SECRET" -hex | awk '{print $2}')"
+
+echo "Calling ${WP_URL}/wp-json/printonet/v1/provision-store"
+echo "tenant_slug=${TENANT_SLUG} request_id=${REQUEST_ID}"
+
+curl -sS -X POST "${WP_URL}/wp-json/printonet/v1/provision-store" \
+  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
+  -H "X-Printonet-Timestamp: ${TIMESTAMP}" \
+  -H "X-Printonet-Signature: ${SIGNATURE}" \
+  --data "$BODY"
+
+echo
*** End Patch

*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: /root/printonet-multitenant/scripts/supplier_pipeline_smoke_test.sh
+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+set -euo pipefail
+
+ENV_FILE="${1:-.env}"
+if [[ ! -f "$ENV_FILE" ]]; then
+  echo "Missing env file: $ENV_FILE"
+  exit 1
+fi
+
+set -a
+source "$ENV_FILE"
+set +a
+
+if [[ -z "${WP_URL:-}" || -z "${PLATFORM_HMAC_SECRET:-}" || -z "${SUPPLIER_WEBHOOK_SECRET:-}" ]]; then
+  echo "WP_URL, PLATFORM_HMAC_SECRET, and SUPPLIER_WEBHOOK_SECRET are required"
+  exit 1
+fi
+
+sign() {
+  local timestamp="$1"
+  local body="$2"
+  printf "%s.%s" "$timestamp" "$body" | openssl dgst -sha256 -hmac "$PLATFORM_HMAC_SECRET" -hex | awk '{print $2}'
+}
+
+signed_post() {
+  local path="$1"
+  local body="$2"
+  local ts
+  ts="$(date +%s)"
+  local sig
+  sig="$(sign "$ts" "$body")"
+
+  curl -sS -X POST "${WP_URL}${path}" \
+    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
+    -H "X-Printonet-Timestamp: ${ts}" \
+    -H "X-Printonet-Signature: ${sig}" \
+    --data "$body"
+}
+
+signed_get() {
+  local path="$1"
+  local body=""
+  local ts
+  ts="$(date +%s)"
+  local sig
+  sig="$(sign "$ts" "$body")"
+
+  curl -sS -X GET "${WP_URL}${path}" \
+    -H "X-Printonet-Timestamp: ${ts}" \
+    -H "X-Printonet-Signature: ${sig}"
+}
+
+SYNC_BODY="$(cat <<JSON
+{
+  "supplier": "printonet_internal",
+  "credentials": {
+    "base_url": "${INTERNAL_SUPPLIER_API_URL}",
+    "api_key": "${INTERNAL_SUPPLIER_API_KEY}"
+  }
+}
+JSON
+)"
+
+echo "Queueing supplier sync..."
+SYNC_RESPONSE="$(signed_post "/wp-json/printonet/v1/suppliers/sync" "$SYNC_BODY")"
+echo "$SYNC_RESPONSE"
+
+JOB_ID="$(python3 - <<'PY' "$SYNC_RESPONSE"
+import json,sys
+try:
+    data=json.loads(sys.argv[1])
+    print(data.get("job_id",""))
+except Exception:
+    print("")
+PY
+)"
+
+if [[ -z "$JOB_ID" ]]; then
+  echo "No job_id returned from sync endpoint"
+  exit 1
+fi
+
+echo "Polling sync status for job_id=${JOB_ID}..."
+for _ in 1 2 3 4 5; do
+  STATUS_RESPONSE="$(signed_get "/wp-json/printonet/v1/suppliers/sync-status/${JOB_ID}")"
+  echo "$STATUS_RESPONSE"
+  STATUS="$(python3 - <<'PY' "$STATUS_RESPONSE"
+import json,sys
+try:
+    data=json.loads(sys.argv[1])
+    print(data.get("job",{}).get("status",""))
+except Exception:
+    print("")
+PY
+)"
+  if [[ "$STATUS" == "success" || "$STATUS" == "failed" ]]; then
+    break
+  fi
+  sleep 2
+done
+
+WEBHOOK_BODY='{"event":"stock_price_update","blog_id":1,"items":[{"sku":"TSHIRT-001","price":"29.99","stock":5}]}'
+echo "Sending supplier webhook delta..."
+curl -sS -X POST "${WP_URL}/wp-json/printonet/v1/suppliers/webhook" \
+  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
+  -H "X-Printonet-Webhook-Secret: ${SUPPLIER_WEBHOOK_SECRET}" \
+  --data "$WEBHOOK_BODY"
+echo
*** End Patch

- In your main backend repo:

content.text:
Wired. I updated the tenant engine to use your Supabase feed contract exactly:

- Calls `GET https://qumrnazgdrijdcihtkah.supabase.co/functions/v1/tenant-catalog-feed?tenant_slug=...&limit=200`
- Signs GET with:
  - `X-Printonet-Timestamp`
  - `X-Printonet-Signature = HMAC_SHA256("${ts}.", PRINTONET_PLATFORM_HMAC_SECRET)`
- No internal API key required
- Added optional `tenant_slug` override support (`PRINTONET_INTERNAL_TENANT_SLUG` / request credential)

I deployed this live and ran smoke tests. Result: auth works, but feed currently returns a non-catalog payload for this tenant (job error: `Invalid internal supplier response: missing items array`; direct feed call returns `tenant_not_found` for `stores.printonet.com`).

So remaining blocker is on feed-side tenant matching, not on VPS integration.  
Please have Lovable map this tenant slug correctly (or tell me the exact expected slug), and return the `items[]` response for that slug; then sync should turn `success` immediately.

[REDACTED]

<timestamp>Wednesday, Apr 29, 2026, 4:06 PM (UTC-5)</timestamp>
<user_query>
The tenant stores will live under the "stores" subdomain based on the name of the store set by the stire owner in the main Printonet platform, for example "greatprints.stores.printonet.com" Is that doable or what do you recommend?
</user_query>

Yes — that’s absolutely doable, and it’s the right model for your platform.

Recommended approach:

- Use `tenant_slug.stores.printonet.com` where `tenant_slug` is generated from store name (slugified), e.g. `Great Prints` -> `greatprints`.
- Keep `stores.printonet.com` as the network/root site, and create each tenant as a multisite subdomain.
- Enforce slug rules in main platform before provisioning:
  - lowercase letters, numbers, hyphens only
  - max length (e.g. 30–40 chars)
  - reserve blocked words (`www`, `admin`, `api`, `support`, `stores`, etc.)
- Handle collisions deterministically:
  - if `greatprints` exists, suggest `greatprints-co`, `greatprints-nyc`, or `greatprints-2`
- Provisioning payload should include both:
  - display name (`store_name`) for branding
  - DNS-safe slug (`tenant_slug`) for domain creation
- Infra requirements:
  - wildcard DNS: `*.stores.printonet.com` -> VPS

### New capabilities added

- **Strict config payload validation**
  - `branding` must be object
  - `layout` must be object and size-limited

    - query `tenant_slug=<store_host>`
    - HMAC headers using existing `PRINTONET_PLATFORM_HMAC_SECRET`
  - Applies updates through same validated/versioned config path

- **Manual pull-sync endpoint**
  - `POST /wp-json/printonet/v1/tenant/pull-sync`
  - Useful for immediate sync/testing from main platform

  - Added `DASHBOARD_CONFIG_FEED_URL` to `.env.example`
  - Installer writes `PRINTONET_DASHBOARD_CONFIG_FEED_URL` into `wp-config.php`

- **Docs updated**

  - `README.md` includes new endpoint

    - `circuit_alert`

- **Status endpoint enriched**

- **Manual pull behavior improved**

  - `pull_sync_interval_minutes: 15`
- Confirmed status endpoint returns:
  - interval `15`
  - fail count `0`
  - circuit closed (`open_until=0`, `alert=false`)
- Verified WordPress cron schedule is now:
  - `printonet_tenant_pull_sync ... recurrence 15 minutes`

### Result

Your tenant engine now has controlled pull cadence + resilience guardrails for dashboard config feed outages, and your main platform can observe sync health via API without shell access.

content.text:
Ran it end-to-end. Current state:

- Provisioning works (`201`): new tenant site created successfully.
- Supplier pipeline works: sync job reached `success`, webhook update succeeded.
- **Only blocker left:** wildcard TLS.

I confirmed the exact issue on tenant URLs:

- `https://smoke-...stores.printonet.com` fails cert validation because current cert only includes:
  - `DNS:stores.printonet.com`
  - (no `*.stores.printonet.com` SAN)

### What needs to happen now (manual DNS challenge)

Because wildcard certs require DNS validation, you (or DNS automation) must complete this once:

```bash
certbot certonly --manual --preferred-challenges dns \
  --agree-tos --manual-public-ip-logging-ok \
  --register-unsafely-without-email \
  -d stores.printonet.com -d "*.stores.printonet.com"
```

Then:

1. Choose **Expand** when prompted.
2. Add the TXT record(s) Certbot asks for in your DNS.
3. Wait for propagation and continue.
4. Update nginx to use the new wildcard cert path if different, then reload:
   - `nginx -t && systemctl reload nginx`

Once that’s done, tenant subdomains will be fully trusted over HTTPS and you’re launch-ready for real tenant URLs.

  -d stores.printonet.com -d "*.stores.printonet.com"
```

Then:

- choose **Expand** when prompted,
- add the TXT record(s) in your DNS provider panel (Hostinger/Cloudflare/etc.),
- wait for propagation, press Enter in terminal to continue.

<user_query>
Where is that the main platform needs to send the new store information?
</user_query>

Pulling the documented API contract and the provisioning route implementation so we can give an accurate answer.

[REDACTED]

The main platform should send **new store creation** to the **multisite root** WordPress REST API, not to a tenant subdomain.

### Endpoint

**`POST https://stores.printonet.com/wp-json/printonet/v1/provision-store`**

(That is the network/root site URL plus `wp-json/printonet/v1/provision-store`.)

Related helpers on the same host:

- **`POST .../wp-json/printonet/v1/slug-availability`** — check/suggest slug before create  
- After the store exists, config sync uses **`POST .../tenant/config`**, **`POST .../tenant/pull-sync`**, etc. (see contract)

### Auth

Signed requests (preferred):

- `X-Printonet-Timestamp` — Unix seconds  
- `X-Printonet-Signature` — `HMAC-SHA256(timestamp + "." + raw_body, PLATFORM_HMAC_SECRET)` (hex)

Optional legacy API key only if `PRINTONET_ALLOW_LEGACY_API_KEY=true`.

### Body (what to send)

JSON including at least **`tenant_slug`** (e.g. `"testo"`) plus store/admin/branding fields as in the contract:

```30:45:/root/printonet-multitenant/docs/API_CONTRACT.md
```json
{
  "tenant_slug": "acme-print",
  "request_id": "optional-idempotency-key-123",
  "store_name": "Acme Print",
  "admin_email": "owner@acmeprint.com",
  "admin_username": "acme_owner",
  "admin_password": "optional-strong-password",
  "primary_color": "#0f172a",
  "secondary_color": "#f8fafc",
  "logo_url": "https://cdn.printonet.com/logos/acme.png",
  "currency": "USD",
  "default_country": "US:CA",
  "weight_unit": "lbs",
  "dimension_unit": "in"
}
```
```

Full reference: `docs/API_CONTRACT.md` in `printonet-multitenant`. Example signing target URL in `docs/HMAC_SIGNING_EXAMPLES.md` uses `https://stores.printonet.com/wp-json/printonet/v1/provision-store`.

**Important:** If the main app posts to anything other than this base URL (wrong host, `http`, or a tenant URL like `testo.stores.printonet.com/...`), provisioning will not hit this handler on the tenant stack.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
It looks like the store data was received, the store url was created, but the provisioing got stuck. Keeps showing "Provisioning: Pending" in the main platform
</user_query>

Tracing the provisioning flow: what marks completion vs. leaves the platform stuck in "Pending".

[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

Here is how this stack behaves and why the main app can stay on **“Provisioning: Pending”** even when the store URL already exists.

### How completion is signaled (tenant engine)

In `printonet-multitenant`, provisioning is **one synchronous HTTP round-trip**. There is **no** separate “provisioning finished” webhook or callback from WordPress to the main platform. When `POST /wp-json/printonet/v1/provision-store` finishes successfully, the engine returns JSON with `success`, `site_id`, `admin_user_id`, and `store_url`:

```163:174:/root/printonet-multitenant/wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core/includes/class-printonet-provisioning-api.php
        $response_data = [
            'success' => true,
            'site_id' => $site_id,
            'admin_user_id' => $admin_user_id,
            'store_url' => 'https://' . $domain,
        ];

        if ($request_id !== '') {
            self::store_request_id_mapping($request_id, $response_data);
        }

        return new WP_REST_Response($response_data, 201);
```

So the **main platform must mark the store provisioned when this response is received and parsed successfully** (and persist `site_id` / `store_url` from the body). Anything else (showing URL early from your own UI, then waiting on something that never comes) will leave **Pending** forever.

### Likely causes of “Pending” with a URL already shown

1. **Wrong HTTP success rule**  
   First-time success is **`201 Created`**. If the same `request_id` is sent again, the API returns **`200`** with `idempotent: true`:

```100:109:/root/printonet-multitenant/wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core/includes/class-printonet-provisioning-api.php
        if ($request_id !== '') {
            $existing = self::find_existing_by_request_id($request_id);
            if (!empty($existing)) {
                return new WP_REST_Response([
                    'success' => true,
                    'idempotent' => true,
                    'site_id' => (int) $existing['site_id'],
                    'admin_user_id' => (int) $existing['admin_user_id'],
                    'store_url' => (string) $existing['store_url'],
                ], 200);
            }
        }
```

If the main app only treats **`200`** or only **`201`** as success, one path will never flip the status.

2. **Client never sees the success response**  
   Timeouts, TLS/proxy errors, or the worker crashing after WordPress already created the site — the URL exists server-side, but your DB row never updates from Pending.

3. **Strict JSON expectations**  
   If the UI builds the URL locally but the job waits for fields that aren’t present or named differently than your client expects, parsing can fail even with HTTP 2xx.

### What to fix on the main platform

- Treat **`2xx` + `success: true`** on **`provision-store`** as “provisioned,” and handle **both `200` (idempotent) and `201` (new)**.  
- Ensure the **same server-side path** that reserves/creates the slug **updates provisioning status** when the HTTP client completes — not a separate step that nothing triggers.  
- Log **`response.status`**, raw body, and errors on that request to confirm whether the client actually receives `201`/`200`.

There is nothing in this repo that pushes provisioning state back to the main app; clearing **Pending** is entirely a **main-platform responsibility** after a successful **`provision-store`** response (or after reconciling with a manual check if the response was lost).

content.text:
Adding an early provisioning check so we fail fast with a clear error if Storefront or the child theme is missing.

[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

input.description: Deploy updated provisioning API to live WP path

content.text:
Here is what was going wrong and what we changed.

### Cause

Provisioning switches each tenant to the **`printonet-storefront` child theme**, which **depends on Automattic’s Storefront parent**. On the server, **Storefront was never installed** — only the child folder existed under `wp-content/themes/`. With a missing parent, WordPress loads **no templates**, so you get a **blank white page** (still HTTP 200).

Nginx logs also showed WooCommerce DB notices on first hits; those were separate. The empty response was from the **broken theme**, not from WooCommerce alone.

### Fix on the server

Storefront was installed with:

`wp theme install storefront`

After that, **`https://alpha.stores.printonet.com/`** returned a normal HTML homepage (~46KB).

### Changes in the repo (so this does not repeat)

1. **`scripts/install_wp_multisite.sh`** — runs **`wp theme install storefront`** before syncing the child theme.
2. **`class-printonet-provisioning-api.php`** — before creating a site, checks that both **`storefront`** and **`printonet-storefront`** exist; if not, provisioning returns **500** with a clear error instead of creating a store that renders blank.

That provisioning update is also copied to **`/var/www/printonet/`** on this machine.

Any other tenant that was created while Storefront was missing should **start working as soon as Storefront is installed** (no need to re-provision). If something still looks wrong on a specific slug, say which URL and we can check it.

content.text: [REDACTED]

### What that message was

**“Store Temporarily Unavailable”** is the title of the internal **`/suspended/`** page used when a store is **suspended** (guests are redirected there). The MU plugin always created that page as **published**, even when **`printonet_tenant_status` was `active`**, so Storefront treated it like any other page and **listed it in menus / navigation**. Your site wasn’t necessarily suspended; the placeholder page was just visible everywhere.

### What we changed

1. **`sync_suspended_page_visibility()`** (replaces `ensure_suspended_page`): creates or updates the page so it is **`draft` while active** and **`publish` only when `status` is `suspended`**, so redirects still work when you suspend a store.

2. **`migrate_legacy_suspended_page()`** on **`init`**: for stores that already had that page published, it **moves it to draft** if the tenant is still active (one request fixes old sites).

The fix is in the repo and copied to `/var/www/printonet/`. For **alpha**, the page is now **draft** and the homepage HTML no longer contains that title.

### Operations note

If you **suspend** a store from the dashboard config (`status: "suspended"`), the page is published again so **`/suspended/`** works for guests. When you set **`active`** again, it goes back to draft and stays out of menus.

Implementing branding: provisioning stores `printonet_*` options but Storefront uses `custom_logo`, `site_icon`, and `storefront_*` theme mods for visible logo, favicon, and accent. Adding a branding helper and wiring provisioning + tenant config to apply them.

[REDACTED]

input.contents:
<?php

if (!defined('ABSPATH')) {
    exit;
}

/**
 * Maps Printonet dashboard colors and asset URLs into WordPress options + Storefront theme mods
 * so logo, favicon, and accent colors actually render on the storefront.
 */
class Printonet_Branding
{
    /**
     * Apply branding keys when present. Safe for partial updates (config sync).
     *
     * @param array $input Keys: primary_color, secondary_color, logo_url, favicon_url (all optional)
     */
    public static function apply(array $input): void
    {
        if (!empty($input['primary_color'])) {
            $primary = self::sanitize_color((string) $input['primary_color'], '#111111');
            update_option('printonet_brand_primary_color', $primary);
            self::apply_primary_to_storefront($primary);
        }

        if (!empty($input['secondary_color'])) {
            $secondary = self::sanitize_color((string) $input['secondary_color'], '#ffffff');
            update_option('printonet_brand_secondary_color', $secondary);
            set_theme_mod('storefront_header_background_color', $secondary);
            set_theme_mod('storefront_footer_background_color', self::soften_background($secondary));
        }

        if (!empty($input['logo_url'])) {
            $url = esc_url_raw((string) $input['logo_url']);
            update_option('printonet_logo_url', $url);
            $id = self::sideload_image_to_media($url, __('Store logo', 'printonet-storefront'));
            if ($id > 0) {
                set_theme_mod('custom_logo', $id);
            }
        }

        if (!empty($input['favicon_url'])) {
            $url = esc_url_raw((string) $input['favicon_url']);
            update_option('printonet_favicon_url', $url);
            $id = self::sideload_image_to_media($url, __('Site icon', 'printonet-storefront'));
            if ($id > 0) {
                update_option('site_icon', $id);
            }
        }
    }

    /**
     * Used during provisioning when blog context is already switched.
     */
    public static function provision_defaults(array $payload): void
    {
        self::apply([
            'primary_color' => $payload['primary_color'] ?? '',
            'secondary_color' => $payload['secondary_color'] ?? '',
            'logo_url' => $payload['logo_url'] ?? '',
            'favicon_url' => $payload['favicon_url'] ?? '',
        ]);
    }

    private static function apply_primary_to_storefront(string $primary): void
    {
        set_theme_mod('storefront_accent_color', $primary);
        set_theme_mod('storefront_header_link_color', $primary);
        set_theme_mod('storefront_button_alt_background_color', $primary);
        set_theme_mod('storefront_button_alt_text_color', '#ffffff');
    }

    private static function soften_background(string $hex): string
    {
        $hex = ltrim($hex, '#');
        if (strlen($hex) !== 6 || !ctype_xdigit($hex)) {
            return '#f0f0f0';
        }
        $r = hexdec(substr($hex, 0, 2));
        $g = hexdec(substr($hex, 2, 2));
        $b = hexdec(substr($hex, 4, 2));
        $mix = static function (int $c): int {
            return (int) round($c * 0.92 + 255 * 0.08);
        };

        return sprintf(
            '#%02x%02x%02x',
            $mix($r),
            $mix($g),
            $mix($b)
        );
    }

    private static function sanitize_color(string $input, string $fallback): string
    {
        $input = trim($input);
        if ($input === '') {
            return $fallback;
        }

        if ($input[0] !== '#') {
            $input = '#' . $input;
        }

        $hex = sanitize_hex_color($input);

        return $hex ?: $fallback;
    }

    /**
     * @return int Attachment ID or 0
     */
    private static function sideload_image_to_media(string $url, string $title): int
    {
        if ($url === '' || !wp_http_validate_url($url)) {
            return 0;
        }

        require_once ABSPATH . 'wp-admin/includes/file.php';
        require_once ABSPATH . 'wp-admin/includes/media.php';
        require_once ABSPATH . 'wp-admin/includes/image.php';

        $tmp = download_url($url);
        if (is_wp_error($tmp)) {
            return 0;
        }

        $path = wp_parse_url($url, PHP_URL_PATH);
        $basename = $path ? sanitize_file_name(basename((string) $path)) : 'image.png';
        if ($basename === '' || $basename === '.') {
            $basename = 'image.png';
        }

        $file_array = [
            'name' => $basename,
            'tmp_name' => $tmp,
        ];

        $id = media_handle_sideload($file_array, 0, $title);

        if (is_wp_error($id)) {
            @unlink($tmp);

            return 0;
        }

        return (int) $id;
    }
}

input.description: Deploy branding changes to live WordPress

content.text: [REDACTED]

Here is what was wrong and what we changed.

### Why logo / favicon / accent did nothing

1. **Storefront does not read `printonet_*` options for the UI**  
   It uses **Customizer theme mods** (`storefront_accent_color`, header/button colors, etc.) and **WordPress core** features for **`custom_logo`** and **`site_icon`** (favicon). We only saved URLs/colors in options and injected `--printonet-*` CSS variables **without** pointing Storefront at them.

2. **Theme mods were tied to the wrong theme**  
   Provisioning called `switch_theme('printonet-storefront')` **after** setting options. Theme mods apply to the **active** theme; new subsites start on Twenty Twenty Five, so nothing was stored for `printonet-storefront`.

3. **Logo / favicon URLs were never imported into Media**  
   Core expects attachment IDs for **Site Logo** and **Site Icon**, not bare URLs.

### What we implemented

- **`Printonet_Branding`** (`class-printonet-branding.php`):  
  - Saves colors into **`printonet_*` options** and maps **primary** → Storefront **`storefront_accent_color`**, **`storefront_header_link_color`**, **`storefront_button_alt_*`**, and **secondary** → header/footer background mods.  
  - **Downloads** `logo_url` / **`favicon_url`** into the Media Library and sets **`custom_logo`** and **`site_icon`**.

- **Provisioning order**: **`switch_theme('printonet-storefront')` runs before branding**, then **`Printonet_Branding::provision_defaults(...)`** so mods attach to the child theme.

- **`favicon_url`** is supported on **`provision-store`** and in **`tenant/config`** branding (documented in `docs/API_CONTRACT.md`).

- **Tenant config** `apply_branding` now delegates to **`Printonet_Branding::apply()`** so pushes/pulls update logo, favicon, and accents the same way.

- **Child theme**: Extra inline CSS on **`printonet-storefront-style`** so prices / block buttons pick up the accent as well.

Deployed copies live under `/var/www/printonet/` on this server.

### What you should do on the main platform

1. Send **`favicon_url`** next to **`logo_url`** on **`provision-store`** (HTTPS URLs the WordPress server can fetch).
2. Ensure **`primary_color` / `secondary_color`** are **`#RRGGBB`** hex (with `#`). Other formats can be rejected by `sanitize_hex_color` and fall back to defaults.

### Stores created before this fix

They still have URLs/colors in options only. Either:

- **`POST /tenant/config`** with the same **`branding`** payload (including **`logo_url`** / **`favicon_url`** / colors), or  
- Trigger a **pull-sync** that returns those fields,

so **`Printonet_Branding`** runs and imports assets + theme mods.

New provisions after deploy pick this up automatically.

input.description: Install Storefront, network-enable themes, activate child on main site

content.text:
Here is what was going on and what we did.

### Naming

**“WooCommerce Storefront”** is the official WooCommerce theme on WordPress.org — slug **`storefront`**. Your stack uses:

- **`storefront`** — parent (WooCommerce Storefront)
- **`printonet-storefront`** — child under `wp-content/themes/printonet-storefront/`

### Script changes (`scripts/install_wp_multisite.sh`)

After installing **`storefront`** and syncing **`printonet-storefront`**, the script now:

1. **`wp theme enable storefront --network`** — allows the parent across the network  
2. **`wp theme enable printonet-storefront --network`** — allows the child for subsites  
3. **`wp theme activate printonet-storefront --url="$WP_URL"`** — turns on the child on the **main** site (`stores.printonet.com`)

Subsites created by provisioning already call `switch_theme('printonet-storefront')`; network-enable avoids theme restrictions for normal site admins where that applies.

### Docs

`README.md` mentions Storefront install + network enable + main-site activation.

### Live server

On **`stores.printonet.com`** these commands were run successfully: Storefront was already installed; both themes are **network enabled**; the active theme on the main site is **`printonet-storefront`**.

<user_query>
I want to make sure that the logo, favicon, color set from the main platform dashboard are assigned and display when creating the store. The text logo should go away and the image logo show. If this is not set up on this side, tell me what to send from the main paltform
</user_query>

Verifying the provisioning → branding pipeline and documenting exact payloads for the main platform.

[REDACTED]

Here’s how this is wired on the **tenant engine** side and what the **main platform** must send.

### Already implemented here (`provision-store`)

After creating the subsite, the engine switches to **`printonet-storefront`** and runs **`Printonet_Branding::provision_defaults()`**, which:

| Dashboard choice | JSON field (exact keys) | What WordPress gets |
|------------------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| Accent / primary | `primary_color` | Storefront accent + links + primary buttons (`storefront_accent_color`, etc.) |
| Secondary panel | `secondary_color` | Header/footer background theme mods |
| Image logo | **`logo_url`** | Image downloaded into Media Library → **`custom_logo`** (Site Logo) |
| Favicon | **`favicon_url`** | Image downloaded → **`site_icon`** (browser tab icon) |

**Text logo vs image logo:** Storefront only shows the **image** when **`custom_logo`** is set (`has_custom_logo()`). If **`logo_url`** is missing, empty, or download fails, there is no custom logo and the **site title text** (`store_name` → blog name) stays visible. There is no separate “text logo” field—text is just the site name when no image logo is applied.

### What to send from the main platform on `POST …/provision-store`

Send these inside the **same JSON body** you sign for HMAC (same string as `timestamp + "." + raw_body`):

```json
{
  "tenant_slug": "your-slug",
  "store_name": "Your Store Name",
  "admin_email": "owner@example.com",
  "primary_color": "#0f172a",
  "secondary_color": "#f8fafc",
  "logo_url": "https://your-cdn.example.com/path/logo.png",
  "favicon_url": "https://your-cdn.example.com/path/favicon.png"
}
```

**Rules:**

1. **Keys must match exactly:** `logo_url`, `favicon_url`, `primary_color`, `secondary_color` (snake_case, not `logoUrl`).
2. **`logo_url` / `favicon_url`:** Public **HTTPS** URLs the **WordPress server can fetch** (no localhost-only URLs). Prefer **PNG, JPG, GIF, WebP**; SVG upload can fail depending on security settings.
3. **`primary_color` / `secondary_color`:** **`#RRGGBB`** hex (with `#`). Values like `rgb(...)` are not accepted and will fall back to defaults.
4. Include **`logo_url`** whenever you want the image logo; omitting it keeps the text site name.

If branding still doesn’t appear after this, typical causes are: wrong JSON keys, non-hex colors, logo URL not reachable from the VPS, or HTTP 200 but non-image body—those prevent sideload and leave the text title.

Reference: ```28:46:/root/printonet-multitenant/docs/API_CONTRACT.md``` and ```60:69:/root/printonet-multitenant/wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core/includes/class-printonet-branding.php```.

input.new_string:
Slug policy:

- lowercase letters, numbers, hyphens only
- max length: 40
- reserved slugs blocked (`www`, `admin`, `api`, `support`, `stores`, etc.)

## Delete store request

`POST /wp-json/printonet/v1/delete-store` — permanently deletes the tenant subsite (database tables dropped). Same HMAC signing as `provision-store`.

```json
{
  "tenant_slug": "acme-print",
  "request_id": "optional-idempotency-key-for-delete"
}
```

Responses:

- **200** — `{ "success": true, "site_id": 12, "tenant_slug": "acme-print" }` after deletion.
- **200** — `{ "success": true, "already_deleted": true, "tenant_slug": "acme-print" }` if that tenant site no longer exists (safe to treat as success when the dashboard retries).
- **200** — `{ "success": true, "idempotent": true, ... }` if `request_id` was already used for a successful delete.
- **403** — attempt to delete the main network site (never allowed).
- **422** — missing `tenant_slug` or invalid slug format.

## Slug availability request

content.text: [REDACTED]

Here’s what was added and how the main platform should use it.

### New endpoint

**`POST https://<network-host>/wp-json/printonet/v1/delete-store`**

- Same **HMAC** rules as `provision-store`: sign `timestamp + "." + raw_json_body` with `PLATFORM_HMAC_SECRET`, headers `X-Printonet-Timestamp` and `X-Printonet-Signature`.
- **Body (JSON):**
  - **`tenant_slug`** (required) — same slug as for provisioning (e.g. `acme-print`).
  - **`request_id`** (optional) — idempotency key for delete; if the same key is sent again after a successful delete, the API returns **`idempotent: true`** without error.

### Responses

| HTTP | Meaning |
|------|--------|
| **200** | `{ "success": true, "site_id": N, "tenant_slug": "..." }` — subsite removed (tables dropped). |
| **200** | `{ "success": true, "already_deleted": true, "tenant_slug": "..." }` — no site for that slug (safe for retries). |
| **200** | `{ "success": true, "idempotent": true, "site_id": ..., "tenant_slug": "..." }` — duplicate delete request id. |
| **403** | Main network site cannot be deleted. |
| **422** | Missing or invalid `tenant_slug`. |

Implementation calls **`wpmu_delete_blog( $id, true )`**, so the tenant’s DB tables are removed.

### Files touched

- `wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core/includes/class-printonet-provisioning-api.php` — route + handler  
- `docs/API_CONTRACT.md`, `docs/HMAC_SIGNING_EXAMPLES.md`, `README.md`  
- `scripts/signed_smoke_test.sh` — provisions then deletes the smoke tenant  

The updated provisioning class is deployed on this server at `/var/www/printonet/`.

### Main platform dashboard

When the user deletes a store, call **`delete-store`** with the stored **`tenant_slug`** (and optionally a fresh **`request_id`** per delete action). Treat **`200`** with **`already_deleted`** as success if you retry after the site is already gone.

input.new_string:
## Tenant config update request

Use this to push dashboard-driven config to each tenant store.

**Targeting:** Either send the request to the tenant origin (e.g. `https://greatprints.stores.printonet.com/...`) **or** send it to the **network base** (`https://stores.printonet.com/...`) and include **`tenant_slug`** in the JSON body (same signing rules). Do not send a `tenant_slug` that disagrees with the request hostname when calling a tenant URL.

```json
{
  "tenant_slug": "greatprints",
  "config_version": 12,
  "pull_sync_interval_minutes": 15,
  "status": "active",
  "branding": {
    "store_name": "Great Prints",
    "logo_url": "https://cdn.printonet.com/logos/greatprints.png",
    "favicon_url": "https://cdn.printonet.com/favicons/greatprints.png",
    "primary_color": "#0f172a",
    "secondary_color": "#f8fafc",
    "font_family": "Inter, sans-serif"
  },
  "woocommerce": {
    "currency": "USD",
    "default_country": "US:CA",
    "weight_unit": "lbs",
    "dimension_unit": "in"
  },
  "layout": {
    "hero_style": "split"
  },
  "homepage_sections": [
    { "id": "hero", "enabled": true },
    { "id": "featured_products", "enabled": true }
  ]
}
```

`status` values:

- `active` — storefront open for purchases (guest policy unchanged).
- `paused` — alias for **`suspended`** (checkout/purchasing blocked for guests; same behavior as suspended).
- `suspended` — checkout/purchasing blocked.

Include **`woocommerce`** only when updating currency, country, or units (same fields as `provision-store`).

input.description: Deploy tenant control + provisioning to live WP

content.text: [REDACTED]

Here is what was added so you can **pause**, **edit**, and **push** from the main dashboard in one consistent way.

### 1. Push from the network URL with `tenant_slug`

You can call the **same signed APIs** on  
`https://stores.printonet.com/wp-json/...`  
and pass **`tenant_slug`** so the engine switches to that subsite before applying config.

Supported:

| Endpoint | How |
|----------|-----|
| **`POST .../tenant/config`** | JSON includes `"tenant_slug": "your-slug"` (removed before applying; not stored as an option). |
| **`GET .../tenant/config`** | Query: **`?tenant_slug=your-slug`** |
| **`POST .../tenant/pull-sync`** | JSON: **`{ "tenant_slug": "your-slug" }`** |
| **`GET .../tenant/pull-sync-status`** | Query: **`?tenant_slug=your-slug`** |

**Safety:** If the request `Host` is a tenant subdomain (e.g. `alpha.stores.printonet.com`), any `tenant_slug` must **match** that host. To target another store, call the **network base** URL with `tenant_slug` in the body/query.

Helper in code: **`Printonet_Provisioning_API::resolve_tenant_blog_id()`**.

### 2. Pause

**`paused`** is accepted as an alias for **`suspended`** (stored internally as `suspended`). Same behavior as before: checkout/purchase blocked for guests, `/suspended/` messaging, etc.

Send either:

- `"status": "paused"`  
- or `"status": "suspended"`

### 3. Edit “original” store attributes + push

**Branding** (same as before): `store_name`, `logo_url`, `favicon_url`, `primary_color`, `secondary_color`, `font_family` — still applied via **`Printonet_Branding`** (theme mods + media).

**New optional block — WooCommerce (parity with provision-store):**

```json
"woocommerce": {
  "currency": "USD",
  "default_country": "US:CA",
  "weight_unit": "lbs",
  "dimension_unit": "in"
}
```

Include **only** keys you want to change. Omitted keys are left as-is.

**GET `tenant/config`** now returns **`tenant_slug`**, **`woocommerce`** (current values), plus existing branding/layout fields.

**Pull sync** from the dashboard feed can also return a **`woocommerce`** object; it is merged through the same `apply_config_payload` path.

### 4. Docs / deploy

- Updated: `docs/API_CONTRACT.md`, `docs/HMAC_SIGNING_EXAMPLES.md`, `README.md`
- Deployed on this server: `class-printonet-tenant-control.php`, `class-printonet-provisioning-api.php`

### Main platform checklist

1. **Pause:** `POST /tenant/config` with `"status": "paused"` (or `"suspended"`) and **`tenant_slug`** when using the network base URL.  
2. **Resume:** same endpoint with `"status": "active"`.  
3. **Edit branding / Woo:** same payload with updated **`branding`** / **`woocommerce`** and bump **`config_version`** when you rely on optimistic concurrency.  
4. Sign the **exact JSON body** (including `tenant_slug`) per `docs/HMAC_SIGNING_EXAMPLES.md`.

input.old_string: - `wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core/` - shared business logic (all tenants); includes **`assets/favicon.ico`** used as the default site icon for every new provision unless `favicon_url` is set
input.new_string:
- `assets/favicon.ico` - source favicon copied into the MU plugin bundle on install; default site icon for every new provision unless `provision-store` sends **`favicon_url`**
- `wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core/` - shared business logic (all tenants)

content.text:
Here’s what was implemented.

### Behavior

- On **`provision-store`**, after applying branding, if **`favicon_url`** is **missing or blank**, the engine imports **`wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core/assets/favicon.ico`** into the new site’s media library and sets **`site_icon`**.
- If the platform sends a non-empty **`favicon_url`**, that still wins (unchanged).
- **`.ico`** uploads are allowed for that import via a short-lived **`upload_mimes`** filter (WordPress often blocks `.ico` by default).

### Files / layout

1. **Bundled asset** (deployed with the MU plugin):  
   `wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core/assets/favicon.ico`  
   — copied from your repo file  
   `printonet-multitenant/assets/favicon.ico`.

2. **`scripts/install_wp_multisite.sh`** — Before rsync, if  
   `${REPO_ROOT}/assets/favicon.ico` exists, it copies into  
   `wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core/assets/favicon.ico` so **`assets/favicon.ico`** stays the single source for installs.

3. **`Printonet_Branding`** — `apply_bundle_default_site_icon()` + `import_local_media_file()` used from **`provision_defaults()`**.

4. **Docs** — `docs/API_CONTRACT.md` and `README.md` updated.

### Live server

`/var/www/printonet/wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core/assets/favicon.ico` and the updated branding class are in place.

**Note:** Existing stores keep whatever favicon they already have; only **new** provisions without **`favicon_url`** get this default. To refresh older tenants, push **`tenant/config`** with a **`favicon_url`** or extend logic later if you want a forced network default.

content.text:
Excellent reference — I’ve built **Theme 1** and wired it as the default for new stores.

### What I implemented

- Created a new theme:  
  - `wp-content/themes/printonet-theme/style.css`  
  - `wp-content/themes/printonet-theme/functions.php`  
  - `wp-content/themes/printonet-theme/front-page.php`

### Visual direction (Patagonia-inspired)

- Large full-width hero with overlaid headline + CTA
- “New Arrivals” horizontal-style product rail feel (card grid)
- Editorial split feature section (image + text block)
- Secondary “Top Picks” visual product grid
- “Latest Stories” post cards
- Dark, bold benefits/footer band
- Accent colors and brand font still driven by your dashboard branding options

### Platform integration updates

- Provisioning now sets new tenant stores to **`printonet-theme`** by default (instead of `printonet-storefront`):
  - updated `class-printonet-provisioning-api.php`
- Installer now deploys and enables the new theme:
  - updated `scripts/install_wp_multisite.sh`
  - now rsyncs `printonet-theme`, network-enables it, and activates it on the main site
- README updated to document the new theme structure and default behavior.

### Deployed on your server

- Theme copied to `/var/www/printonet/wp-content/themes/printonet-theme/`
- Provisioning class updated live
- Theme enabled network-wide and activated on `stores.printonet.com`
- Theme list confirms:
  - `printonet-theme` = active
  - `storefront` = parent
  - `printonet-storefront` = still available (inactive)

---

If you want, next I can do a **polish pass** to make this even closer to your screenshot:
- tighter spacing/typography system
- sticky minimal header
- horizontal slider behavior for rails
- more premium product-card hover motion and image crops

input.command:
python3 - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
p=Path('/root/printonet-multitenant/wp-content/themes/printonet-theme/functions.php')
s=p.read_text()
old="""    wp_enqueue_style(
        'printonet-theme-style',
        get_stylesheet_uri(),
        ['storefront-style'],
        wp_get_theme()->get('Version')
    );
"""
new="""    $style_path = get_stylesheet_directory() . '/style.css';
    $style_version = file_exists($style_path) ? (string) filemtime($style_path) : wp_get_theme()->get('Version');

    wp_enqueue_style(
        'printonet-theme-style',
        get_stylesheet_uri(),
        ['storefront-style'],
        $style_version
    );
"""
s=s.replace(old,new)
p.write_text(s)
print('ok')
PY

content.text:
You were right — root cause found and fixed.

### Why nothing changed
Two issues were blocking it:

- You were likely viewing stores still on the old theme (`printonet-storefront`), while width changes were in `printonet-theme`.
- CSS was cached because stylesheet version stayed `0.1.0`.

### What I fixed
- Activated `printonet-theme` on **all existing stores**:
  - `stores.printonet.com`
  - `smoke-1777498427.stores.printonet.com`
  - `alpha.stores.printonet.com`
  - `royal.stores.printonet.com`
- Added cache-busting in `printonet-theme/functions.php`:
  - stylesheet version now uses `filemtime(style.css)` so CSS changes apply immediately.
- Your `1440px` wrapper override is already in place.

You should now see the width change. If you tell me which exact store URL you’re viewing, I can verify that page’s loaded CSS live and tune spacing next.

content.text:
Found it. There were **two separate issues**:

1. **Tenant routing bug on this side (fixed)**
- `/wp-json/printonet/v1/suppliers/sync` was always queuing jobs on the current blog (usually main site), so tenant stores stayed empty.
- I patched it to accept **`tenant_slug`** and queue the job for that tenant’s blog.
- Deployed to production.

2. **Upstream supplier feed error (current blocker)**
- After routing fix, I triggered sync for `royal` and it still failed.
- The internal supplier feed response is:
  - `{"error":"db_error","detail":"column corporate_stores.instawp_site_url does not exist"}`
- So your main/internal feed endpoint is failing before returning `items`, which is why no products are inserted.

---

### What main platform should send now (for tenant sync)

Call:

- `POST https://stores.printonet.com/wp-json/printonet/v1/suppliers/sync`

Body:

```json
{
  "supplier": "printonet_internal",
  "tenant_slug": "royal",
  "credentials": {}
}
```

(Use your normal HMAC headers.)

---

### Why products still don’t show

Even with correct tenant routing, product sync can’t proceed until the internal feed returns a valid payload like:

```json
{ "items": [ ... ] }
```

Right now it returns DB error due to missing column `corporate_stores.instawp_site_url`. Once that feed query is fixed upstream, products should start populating tenant stores.

content.text:
You were hitting **two separate issues**, both now fixed on WP side:

- **Shop page hidden by WooCommerce “coming soon” mode** (`woocommerce_coming_soon = yes`)
- **Product imports were not attaching featured images** from the supplier `image` field

### What I changed

1. **Disabled coming-soon mode** on `royal` and in defaults for future stores:
   - `woocommerce_coming_soon = no`
   - `woocommerce_feature_site_visibility_badge_enabled = no`

2. **Updated `printonet_internal` sync importer** to:
   - read `description` + `image` from feed items
   - update product content/title
   - sideload image and set `_thumbnail_id` (featured image) with admin-user context

3. Re-ran sync for `royal`.

### Current verification

- Shop HTML now renders products (not empty):
  - found **12** product cards on `/shop/`
- Featured images are now being set:
  - **52 published products with thumbnails** in `royal`
- You may still see placeholder on some products where feed image is missing/invalid for that specific item.

If you want, next I can run a cleanup pass to remove/merge earlier duplicate products and keep only one row per SKU so the catalog looks cleaner.

content.text:
Fixed — both issues are addressed.

### What was wrong

1. **Images still looked cut off**  
   `New Arrivals` cards were accidentally not using the `--product` contain class.

2. **Bestsellers not showing**  
   After deduping products, there weren’t enough products to satisfy the old slice (`array_slice($products, 13, 5)`), so that section became empty.

### What I changed

- Ensured product-card class is applied to the intended product rows:
  - New Arrivals
  - Trail Essentials
  - Top Picks
  - Bestsellers
- Removed that class from `Shop by Collection` (category cards), so only product rows use contain behavior.
- Added fallback logic so `Trail Essentials` and `Bestsellers` always show items even with small catalogs.
- Improved contain rendering frame:
  - fixed link block wrapper
  - explicit image height in product cards (`clamp(...)`) + `object-fit: contain`

### Verified

- Homepage HTML now includes `Bestsellers` and 5 cards in that section.
- Product rows consistently use `printonet-card--product`.

Deployed to live theme. Hard refresh once to clear cached CSS.

content.text:
You were right — I traced and fixed it on the WordPress side.

### Root cause
New-store sync jobs were failing with:
- `Invalid internal supplier response: missing items array`

The bug was in `printonet-core`:
- `tenant_slug` was saved in the job payload
- but the internal supplier adapter only read `tenant_slug` from `credentials`
- so sync requests for new tenants were sent with the wrong tenant identity

### Fix applied
Updated:
- `wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core/includes/class-printonet-supplier-sync.php`

Change:
- In `run_sync()`, copy payload `tenant_slug` into `$credentials['tenant_slug']` before calling `fetch_catalog()`.

### Verification
- Deployed MU plugin patch.
- Re-ran failed jobs for the new store (`blog_id=6`).
- Jobs `17`, `18`, `19` are now `success`.
- Tenant now has products: `tenant6_products: 13`.

If products still don’t appear in your browser immediately, do a hard refresh once (`Ctrl+Shift+R`).

[REDACTED]

content.text:
Fixed. The underlying issue was sync timing — products were often imported *after* store creation, so it looked like nothing was pushed.

### What I changed
In `wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core/includes/class-printonet-supplier-sync.php`:

- `status: "success"` in API response
- products are present right away (verified on tenant)

This should stop the “new store has no products” experience after pushes.

input.name: Main platform repo access
input.overview: You can work on the main Printonet platform from this environment by cloning that GitHub repo into a path Cursor can open, using normal git auth (SSH or HTTPS + PAT). One chat is usually tied to one workspace root, so either open that repo or use a multi-root workspace.
input.plan:
# Connect main-platform GitHub work to this Cursor session

## What is (and is not) possible

- **Possible:** I make edits, run tests, commit, and push **in any Git repo that exists on this machine** and is **inside the Cursor workspace** (or opened as the workspace).
- **Not automatic:** There is no separate “link GitHub” step that grants repo access without **git clone + credentials** (SSH key or HTTPS token) on this environment.

## Practical setups (pick one)

### Option A — Open main platform as its own Cursor project (simplest)

1. Clone the main-platform repo on this machine (same server or your dev box where Cursor runs).
2. In Cursor: **File → Open Folder** → select that repo root.
3. Start (or continue) a chat **with that folder as the workspace** — instructions apply only to files under that root.

**Pros:** Clean separation, no path confusion.  
**Cons:** You switch between two Cursor windows/projects for multitenant vs main platform.

### Option B — Multi-root workspace (both repos in one window)

1. Clone main-platform next to this repo (e.g. sibling directories).
2. Cursor: add both folders to a **multi-root workspace** (`.code-workspace`).
3. One chat can still bias toward a **primary** folder; be explicit which repo you mean (“change `main-platform/…`”).

**Pros:** Single window.  
**Cons:** Easy to edit the wrong repo unless paths are explicit.

### Option C — Monorepo / submodule (only if you want one Git history)

Move or submodule the multitenant engine under the main-platform repo (or the reverse). **Larger process** — only if you want long-term repo unification.

## GitHub authentication (required for push)

- **SSH:** Add deploy key or personal SSH key to GitHub; clone `git@github.com:org/repo.git`.
- **HTTPS:** Clone with PAT (stored via credential helper); pushes use the same.

Without one of these, I can still **read/edit locally** after you clone, but **push** will fail until auth is configured.

## Flow after setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  subgraph cursor [Cursor workspace]
    clone[Local clone of main-platform]
    edits[Agent edits and tests]
  end
  subgraph github [GitHub]
    remote[origin]
  end
  clone --> edits
  edits -->|git push| remote
```

##What you need to provide (once)

- **Repo URL** (SSH or HTTPS).
- **Where** it should live on disk (path).
- **Auth method** you prefer (SSH vs PAT).

## Copy/paste between two AIs

Once main-platform is **this workspace** (or a root in a multi-root workspace), you don’t need to paste multitenant instructions into the “other” builder for cross-repo tasks — you’d paste **only** main-platform-specific context, or run everything in one project.

No code changes to [printonet-multitenant](.) are required for this; it is purely **workspace + git + clone** setup on your side (or on this host).

todos.content: Choose Option A (separate Cursor project), B (multi-root), or C (monorepo/submodule)
Clone main-platform repo and configure SSH or HTTPS+PAT for push
Open repo in Cursor or add to .code-workspace with multitenant if desired
Use explicit paths/repo names in prompts when multi-root

content.text:
<user_query>
I have cloned the repo to a folder in my local. What do I do next?
</user_query>

Here’s what to do next after the clone:

### 1. Open it in Cursor
- **File → Open Folder…** (or **Open…** on Mac)  
- Pick the **root folder** of the cloned repo (the one that contains `.git`).

That makes this chat’s workspace the main platform so edits and searches apply there.

### 2. Confirm git remote and branch
In Cursor’s terminal (inside that folder):

```bash
cd /path/to/your/main-platform-repo
git remote -v
git status
git branch
```

You want `origin` pointing at GitHub and a clean working tree (or the branch you develop on).

### 3. Make sure you can push
- **SSH:** `ssh -T git@github.com` should succeed.  
- **HTTPS:** first `git pull` / `git push` may prompt for auth; use a **PAT** if GitHub asks for a password.

If push fails, fix credentials before relying on the agent to push.

### 4. Point future chats at this project
Start new Composer/Agent sessions **with this folder open**, or say explicitly: “workspace is `main-platform` at `…`”.

### 5. Optional: both repos in one window
If you want multitenant + main platform together:

- **File → Add Folder to Workspace…** → add the second repo.  
- Save as **File → Save Workspace As…** → `something.code-workspace`.

Then in prompts, name paths clearly (e.g. `main-platform/src/...` vs `printonet-multitenant/...`).

---

**Bottom line:** Open the cloned folder as the Cursor workspace (or add it to a multi-root workspace). After that, you can describe tasks here and I’ll change that repo directly—no need to copy instructions to another AI unless that other session is still pointed at a different folder.

If you tell me your OS and whether you use SSH or HTTPS for GitHub, I can narrow step 3 to exactly what to run.

content.text:
Here’s what’s done and why the push stopped.

### Done on this machine

1. **Git was wrong before** — `.git` lived under `/root`, so it was tracking parent junk (Cursor, `.bash_history`, etc.). **`printonet-multitenant` had no repo.**

2. **New repo only inside** [`/root/printonet-multitenant`](/root/printonet-multitenant):
   - `git init` on branch **`main`**
   - Added [`.gitignore`](/root/printonet-multitenant/.gitignore): ignores `.env`, root `/*.html` scrapes, and **`assets/prototype theme/`** (~258 MB reference bundle so GitHub stays lean)
   - Initial commit: **41 files** (MU plugin, themes, docs, scripts, assets, `.env.example`)

3. **Remote added:**  
   `origin` → `https://github.com/victormayala/printonet-multitenant-wp.git`

4. **Push failed** — GitHub needs credentials; this environment has **no GitHub user/token or SSH key** configured:
   ```text
   fatal: could not read Username for 'https://github.com': No such device or address
   ```

### What you should do next (pick one)

**A. Push from your laptop (simplest)**  
Open your existing clone of `printonet-multitenant-wp`, then either:

- Replace contents with a copy of this tree and commit/push, **or**
- Add this server as a remote only if you’ve copied the repo here — easier: **pull the empty GitHub repo, copy files in, commit, push** with your normal GitHub login.

**B. Push from this server**  
Configure auth once, then:

```bash
cd /root/printonet-multitenant
git push -u origin main
```

Use either:

- **HTTPS + PAT** (classic token with `repo` scope), or  
- **SSH**: add a deploy key or your SSH key to GitHub and  
  `git remote set-url origin git@github.com:victormayala/printonet-multitenant-wp.git`

**C. Optional cleanup**  
If you don’t need the accidental repo at `/root/.git`, remove it so `git` commands run from `/root` don’t confuse you:

```bash
rm -rf /root/.git
```

Only do that if you’re sure nothing important lives only in that repo.

---

I **cannot** complete the GitHub login from here without you supplying a PAT or SSH access (don’t paste secrets in chat). Once auth is set on this host or you push from your Mac, `git push -u origin main` will upload the commit that’s already prepared in `/root/printonet-multitenant`.

input.name: WP DB and editing model
input.overview: Store data for tenants already lands in WordPress when provisioning, config sync, and supplier sync succeed—but most of it is written via REST APIs and cron jobs, not through wp-admin screens. Future risk is not “missing DB writes” but unclear ownership when merchants edit in WordPress vs the main platform overwrites them.
input.plan:
# WordPress DB vs dashboard: what is actually happening

## Short answer

**Data does hit the WordPress database** for this multitenant design, but **little of it is created through traditional wp-admin “building” flows**. The main platform and MU plugin treat WordPress as the **runtime store** (subsites, WooCommerce catalog rows, options), while the **dashboard** is often the **control plane**.

If products or settings looked missing at times, that was typically **pipeline/sync or routing bugs** (e.g. jobs targeting wrong blog, malformed supplier payload), not an architectural choice to bypass MySQL.

## What writes into WordPress today

| Area | How it gets into WP | Where it lives |
|------|---------------------|----------------|
| New store | `wpmu_create_blog`, user linkage, theme activation, branding | Multisite tables + per-blog `wp_*options` / theme mods |
| Branding / tenant settings | `POST /tenant/config` (and pull-sync) via [class-printonet-tenant-control.php](printonet-multitenant/wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core/includes/class-printonet-tenant-control.php) | `update_option(...)` e.g. `printonet_tenant_status`, `printonet_brand_*`, Woo options, layout JSON |
| Product catalog | Supplier sync → `upsert_simple_product` in [class-printonet-supplier-sync.php](printonet-multitenant/wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core/includes/class-printonet-supplier-sync.php) | `wp_posts` (`product`), post meta (`_sku`, prices, stock), media for images |

So: **orders, customers, and cart behavior** still use normal WooCommerce once shoppers use the storefront; **catalog rows** are intended to exist as real products in the DB after sync.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  subgraph main [Main platform]
    dash[Dashboard UI]
    cat[Catalog / supplier feed]
  end
  subgraph wp [WordPress tenant blog]
    api[REST printonet-core]
    woo[WooCommerce DB]
  end
  dash -->|provision_store tenant_config| api
  cat -->|suppliers_sync| api
  api --> woo
```

## Why it can *feel* like “nothing is built in wp-admin”

- **No manual product entry**: Merchants may never open **Products → Add** if the main platform is the source of truth and sync fills the catalog.
- **Config is API-driven**: Branding and flags live in options updated by signed APIs, not only by checking boxes in **Settings**.
- **Visibility**: Inspecting the **network** admin vs the **tenant** site’s database prefix matters; product counts are per `blog_id`.

## Future risk (the real one)

The fragile part is **not** “WordPress has no data” if sync works—it is **who owns edits**:

- If the main platform **re-syncs** and overwrites SKUs/prices/titles, **changes made only in wp-admin** can be lost unless you define rules (e.g. “platform wins”, “WP wins for field X”, “two-way sync”).
- If you add **more** main-platform screens without syncing down, WordPress and the platform can **drift**.

## Do you have to build custom edit pages on the main platform?

**No, that is not the only solution.** Common patterns:

1. **WordPress-first for merchant ops**  
   Give store admins **wp-admin / WooCommerce** for day-to-day catalog and content; main platform only provisions, pushes **defaults**, and periodic catalog sync with a clear **conflict policy**.

2. **Platform-first (what you have leaned toward)**  
   Main platform is canonical; WordPress is a **rendering + checkout engine**; merchant-facing “edit store” lives on the platform (or limited WP role).

3. **Hybrid**  
   Platform: identity, billing, **policy**, global catalog. WordPress: **merchandising** (categories, homepage blocks, optional local products) with selective sync fields.

## Recommendations so this is not an issue later

- **Document ownership**: For each entity (product, price, stock, branding, pages), state **source of truth** and **overwrite behavior** on sync.
- **Operational checks**: Monitor `wp_printonet_sync_jobs` and tenant `product` counts; alert on failed jobs (you already have status fields).
- **Merchant UX**: Decide explicitly: **either** train merchants on wp-admin for edits **or** invest in platform UI and **stop** treating wp-admin as authoritative for those fields.

No code changes are required to answer this question; next step is a **product decision** on edit ownership, then optional implementation (webhooks, selective sync, or “read-only catalog in WP” flags).

todos.content: Document source-of-truth per entity (catalog, branding, pages) and sync overwrite rules
Choose platform-first vs WP-admin-first vs hybrid for merchant edits
Add alerts/dashboard for sync job failures and empty-catalog tenants

input.new_string:
## Clarification: are we recommending main-platform UI for merchant edits?

**No — not as a universal requirement.** The plan is to **pick one model deliberately**, not to assume every change must be built in the main platform.

- **Main-platform UI** makes sense when: you want a single branded app, role-based access, audit logs, and the **main platform is the only source of truth** for catalog/branding (WordPress is a headless-style runtime + checkout).
- **WordPress (wp-admin) UI** makes sense when: you want to ship faster for merchant self-service, lean on WooCommerce’s built-in product/order tools, and accept **training merchants on WP** (with sync rules so platform pushes do not surprise them).
- **Hybrid** makes sense when: platform owns **policy and shared catalog**, but tenants customize **merchandising, pages, or local assortments** in WP—with explicit fields that sync never overwrites.

So: **we are not recommending “you must build all edit screens on the main platform.”** We *are* recommending that **somewhere** there is a clear story for “who edits what” (platform, WP, or split), so you do not end up with silent overwrites or drift.

## Do you have to build custom edit pages on the main platform?

**No, that is not the only solution.** Common patterns:

### Decision (v1)

Document which fields the platform owns so wp-admin changes are either discouraged or treated as non-authoritative for those fields.

content.text:
**No.** We are **not** saying every change has to live only on the main platform. The recommendation is to **choose deliberately** who owns edits (platform, wp-admin, or a split), so sync does not silently wipe merchant work.

For **your v1 choice (platform-first)**, that **does** mean: treat the **main platform as canonical** for catalog/branding (and build merchant-facing edit UI there where it matters), and treat WordPress primarily as **storefront + checkout**, with clear rules that platform-driven sync **wins** for those fields unless you later carve out exceptions.

input.new_string:
### Decision (v1)

**Platform-first for catalog and tenant-wide policy:** the main platform is canonical for **supplier-driven products** (SKU, core pricing/stock rules as defined by sync) and for **API-pushed tenant options** where you choose to enforce consistency.

**WordPress-admin for CMS and merchandising surfaces:** merchants are **expected** to use wp-admin (or the Site Editor / theme-provided flows) for **pages, hero/banner content, navigation, and local promotions** that are not global catalog rows—provided you **draw a hard boundary** so scheduled sync and `POST /tenant/config` do not overwrite those surfaces.

Together this is **platform-first + hybrid content**: same stack, two ownership zones.

## Plan when merchants edit in wp-admin (heroes, promotions, pages)

This is a normal extension of v1. The work is **policy + boundaries + merchant docs**, not “pick platform OR WordPress.”

### 1. Ownership matrix (document before scaling merchants)

| Surface | Recommended owner (this scenario) | Risk if unclear |
|---------|-----------------------------------|-----------------|
| **Products** (`product` post type, SKU/meta from supplier) | Platform / sync | Merchant edits in WC admin overwritten on sync unless you add “local override” rules |
| **Pages, posts, reusable blocks** | **WordPress** | Low conflict if sync never touches `page`/`post` |
| **Hero / homepage sections** | **WordPress** (Customizer, template parts, block patterns, or theme options **not** overwritten by API) | High if `tenant/config` or layout JSON **replaces** entire homepage structure each sync |
| **Menus** | **WordPress** | Same as above |
| **Media library** (banner images) | **WordPress** | Sync product images should only attach to products, not bulk-delete unrelated attachments |
| **Promotions** | Choose one: **Woo Coupons** / cart rules in WP **or** platform-owned campaigns pushed to WP | Drift if both places define promos with no precedence rule |

Add this table (or equivalent) to internal docs and a short **merchant-facing** guide: “Edit your homepage and pages here; don’t rely on manual product edits for catalog SKUs.”

### 2. Technical guardrails (what engineering must guarantee)

- **Scope supplier sync** to `product` (and product-linked meta/media). Audit [class-printonet-supplier-sync.php](printonet-multitenant/wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core/includes/class-printonet-supplier-sync.php) for any broad deletes or option writes that could hit content.
- **Scope `POST /tenant/config`** in [class-printonet-tenant-control.php](printonet-multitenant/wp-content/mu-plugins/printonet-core/includes/class-printonet-tenant-control.php): separate **platform-controlled keys** from **merchant-controlled keys**. Prefer **merge** for layout JSON (e.g. only update keys the platform owns) rather than replacing entire blobs—**if** merchants customize layout in WP-backed storage.
- **Theme contract**: define how heroes work (e.g. front page template + patterns, or dedicated options keys). If the theme reads hero config from **options**, decide whether those keys are **excluded** from platform pushes once the store is live.
- **Roles**: use **Shop Manager** / **Editor** caps so merchants can manage content without installing plugins or editing theme PHP.

### 3. Promotions — explicit product choice

- **WP-native:** WooCommerce coupons, sale prices on products (conflicts with sync if sync resets prices), or third-party promo plugins—document whether sale price is sync-managed.
- **Platform-native:** campaigns defined on main app, rendered via API or synced blocks into WP—more control, more build work.

Pick one **system of record** for “store-wide promo logic”; use the other only as display if needed.

### 4. Operational follow-up

Same as the rest of the plan: monitor sync failures and empty catalogs; add alerts so merchants are not “fixing” silent catalog issues only in wp-admin.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph platform [Main platform]
    cat[Catalog sync]
    cfg[Tenant config API]
  end
  subgraph wp [WordPress tenant]
    products[Products Woo]
    pages[Pages posts blocks]
    promos[Coupons or synced campaigns]
  end
  cat -->|product rows only| products
  cfg -->|selected options merge| wp
  pages -->|merchant authored| pages
  promos -->|policy chosen| promos
```
