content.text:
Here is the plan for **merchants editing in wp-admin** (heroes, promotions, new pages) while staying aligned with **platform-first catalog**.

### How it fits v1

You do **not** have to move all of that to the main platform. Treat it as **two ownership zones**:

1. **Platform / sync** — canonical for **supplier-driven catalog** (products, SKUs, and whatever fields you sync from the main platform).
2. **WordPress** — canonical for **CMS-style work**: **pages**, **homepage / hero content**, **menus**, **media for banners**, and **promotions**—once you decide *where* promotions live (see below).

That is **platform-first + hybrid content**, not a contradiction.

### Concrete plan (policy + guardrails)

1. **Write an ownership matrix** — For each area (products vs pages vs hero vs coupons), document **source of truth** and **what sync/config is allowed to overwrite**. Tell merchants clearly: e.g. “Use wp-admin for homepage and pages; don’t expect hand-edited catalog fields to win over sync.”
2. **Keep sync narrow** — Supplier sync should **only** touch **products** (and product-linked meta/images), not bulk-change pages or unrelated options.
3. **Keep tenant config narrow or merge safely** — If `POST /tenant/config` or layout JSON **replaces** whole homepage settings, wp-admin hero work gets wiped. Prefer **only updating keys the platform owns**, or **excluding** merchant-controlled keys after go-live.
4. **Theme contract** — Decide how heroes are stored (blocks, template parts, customizer options). Align that with the keys sync/config may write.
5. **Promotions — pick one system of record** — Either **Woo coupons / WP-side rules** (simpler for merchants, watch conflicts with sync on **sale prices**) **or** **platform-defined campaigns** pushed into WP (more control, more build). Avoid both defining the same promo without precedence rules.

I added this as a full section in [wp_db_and_editing_model_2c9b4d6c.plan.md](/root/.cursor/plans/wp_db_and_editing_model_2c9b4d6c.plan.md), refined the v1 decision to include **WP-admin for CMS/merchandising**, expanded the **doc-ownership** todo (heroes, promos, confirm sync scope), and updated recommendations with an **implementation order**.

If you want to lock one detail in the plan next: should **promotions** default to **Woo coupons in WP** or **campaigns owned on the platform**?

## Plan when merchants edit in wp-admin (heroes, promotions, pages)
input.new_string:
Together this is **platform-first + hybrid content**: same stack, two ownership zones.

## Alternative operating model: you only “own” provision + product sync

**Scenario:** The multitenant platform **deploys** the store, **runs product sync** into WooCommerce, and offers **payment setup** (your aggregated payment vendor **or** the merchant’s own / BYO gateway). **Store customization** (theme, pages, heroes, merchandising, most Woo settings) is **the merchant’s responsibility**; your product obligation is **catalog correctness and availability**, not bespoke storefront work.

This is consistent with the architecture: WordPress remains the **tenant-operated storefront**; the platform is the **host + catalog pipeline + optional payments facilitator**.

### What you should still define in writing

| Topic | Platform typical responsibility | Merchant typical responsibility |
|-------|--------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Store creation, DNS, base theme** | Provision subsite, defaults, access URLs | Custom content, navigation, homepage |
| **Product catalog** | Sync SKUs, prices/stock per your rules | Not relying on manual product edits if sync overwrites them |
| **Payments** | If **platform vendor**: enable/configure per contract; if **BYO**: document connection steps, creds storage, **who is merchant of record** | Valid gateway account, webhooks, dispute handling per chosen model |
| **Compliance / PCI** | Depends on model: aggregated PSP vs BYO shifts liability and what you must certify | BYO: merchant + their PSP relationship |
| **Support** | Incidents in **sync jobs**, **provision APIs**, **your payment integration** | WP plugins, custom CSS, third-party themes, content bugs |

### Technical implications (so “merchant owns customization” is true)

- **`POST /tenant/config`**: Prefer **initial provisioning + optional policy flags** only, or **merge** updates that do **not** clobber merchant-controlled options/theme mods/layout. If you keep pushing full branding/layout from the platform after go-live, you are **not** hands-off on customization—you are still co-authoring the storefront.
- **Supplier sync**: Keep scoped to **products**; document that **manual product changes may be overwritten** if sync is authoritative—merchants customize **presentation**, not **canonical catalog fields**, unless you add local overrides.
- **Payment dual mode**: Implement as explicit tenant flags (e.g. `payment_mode: platform_psp | byo`) and Woo gateway configuration; ensure secrets for BYO live in **tenant-scoped** storage and are **never** overwritten by unrelated sync.
- **Monitoring**: Still your ops problem for **empty catalog / failed sync** (merchant will blame the store even if customization is theirs).

### How this relates to earlier UX choices

This model **does not** require main-platform UI for heroes/pages; it **expects** wp-admin (or agency) customization. It **reinforces** clear docs: **platform SLA = provision + catalog pipeline (+ chosen payment path)**; **merchant = everything else** unless you sell add-on managed services.

## Plan when merchants edit in wp-admin (heroes, promotions, pages)

content.text:
That model is coherent: **the platform is responsible for spinning up the tenant, keeping the catalog in sync, and (optionally) payment plumbing**; **the merchant owns storefront customization**. It matches WordPress as a **tenant-operated** storefront, not a fully managed site-builder product—unless you choose to push branding/layout from the platform forever.

**What that implies in practice**

1. **Product sync** — You treat **WooCommerce products** as **platform-owned data** for the fields you sync. Merchants customize **theme, pages, menus, banners**; they should not assume hand-edited SKUs/prices survive unless you document overrides. Your operational focus stays **sync health + monitoring** (still your problem when the catalog is empty).

2. **`tenant/config` and branding** — If you truly want “customization is on them,” **avoid repeatedly overwriting** theme mods, layout JSON, or options that merchants change—otherwise you’re still controlling the storefront from the platform. Prefer **bootstrap defaults at provision**, then **merge-only or merchant-owned keys** after go-live.

3. **Payments (yours vs theirs)** — Treat this as an explicit **mode per tenant** (e.g. platform PSP vs BYO gateway). Document **who configures credentials**, **who is merchant of record**, and **support boundaries** (your bugs vs their gateway account). BYO usually shifts more compliance/support surface to the merchant.

4. **Support / SLA** — Put in customer-facing terms: **you own provision + catalog pipeline + the integration you provide**; **they own WP content, plugins they add, and customization**—unless you sell managed storefront work separately.

I added this as **“Alternative operating model: you only own provision + product sync”** plus a **support-boundary** todo in [wp_db_and_editing_model_2c9b4d6c.plan.md](/root/.cursor/plans/wp_db_and_editing_model_2c9b4d6c.plan.md), including the table and technical notes so it stays aligned with sync scope and `tenant/config` behavior.

input.query: Where does store provisioning create pages, menus, or default WordPress content?

content.text: [REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

Here’s a practical read based on how your stack behaves today and what WordPress/WooCommerce normally expect.

## Already close

- **Products** — Supplier sync creates real WooCommerce products (`wp_posts` + meta), so **Products** in wp-admin can look like a hand-built catalog **once sync has finished** (and stays healthy).
- **Branding** — Logo/colors/favicon land via provisioning/branding, so **Appearance** can already reflect the store identity.
- **Homepage content (partially)** — `printonet_homepage_sections` and layout options can drive the live site, but that is **stored as options/JSON**, not necessarily as a pile of **Pages** the merchant sees listed under **Pages** unless you mirror it.

## What “looks manually built” in wp-admin usually still needs

| Area | Difficulty | Notes |
|------|------------|--------|
| **WooCommerce system pages** (Shop, Cart, Checkout, My Account, policies) | **Low–medium** | Often created when WooCommerce **runs its installer** on that blog. Provisioning today mainly sets **options** (`apply_default_woo_settings`); it does **not** obviously run a full WC install for the new site. You’d verify network/site activation and either rely on WC’s install or **explicitly** ensure pages + `woocommerce_*` page IDs exist. |
| **Primary navigation menu** (Home, Shop, Cart, key pages) | **Medium** | Straightforward with `wp_create_nav_menu` + assigning to **Storefront / child** menu locations; theme-specific details and testing per theme. |
| **Starter pages** (About, Contact, FAQ, hero as page, etc.) | **Low** if plain HTML/block placeholders; **medium–high** if you want polished block markup matching your theme | Loop over templates + `wp_insert_post`; richer layouts mean patterns, template parts, or block JSON. |
| **Reading settings** (front page = static page) | **Low** | `update_option('show_on_front', 'page')` + page IDs. |
| **Categories/collections in admin** | **Variable** | Your supplier sync path sets `product_type`; **product categories** as merchants expect may need explicit taxonomy sync or defaults if the catalog is flat today. |
| **Timing / perception** | **Operational** | If sync is queued, a merchant who logs in **immediately** may still see an empty catalog until jobs finish—needs clear expectation or “ready” signaling. |

So: **getting to “full catalog + sensible Woo shell” is moderate effort**. Getting to **“every list in wp-admin mirrors a lovingly hand-crafted site”** (menus, many pages, rich blocks, collections) is **more product/design work** than hard engineering—mostly **content templates + QA across tenants**.

## Reality check on “everything aligned”

- **Front vs back** will match if you drive both from the **same sources** (products from sync, navigation from seeded menus, homepage from either real pages or options your theme reads consistently).
- **Options-driven homepage** without mirroring into Pages can make the **front look finished** while **Pages** still feel sparse—if “aligned” means **what they see under Pages/Menus matches the storefront**, you should **seed pages/menus explicitly**, not only JSON options.

**Bottom line:** Delivering a store where wp-admin **looks credible and consistent** (products + WC pages + primary menu + a handful of pages + correct reading settings) is **very doable in a focused sprint** once WC install/menu seeding is nailed. Chasing **perfect parity** with a manually designer-built site is **ongoing content/template work**, not a one-time toggle.

If you want this as a concrete backlog: (1) guarantee WC pages per new blog, (2) seed primary menu from a manifest, (3) seed standard pages + homepage assignment, (4) optional category strategy for catalog, (5) gate “store ready” on sync completion for product count.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
Should we start doing it now or after we finish building the theme look and feel?
</user_query>

**Defer the *visual* and *navigation-shaped* seeding until the theme’s look, content model, and menu locations are stable.** Do **not** defer everything—some work is safe and useful now.

**Do now (theme‑agnostic)**

- **Catalog pipeline** — reliable product sync, monitoring, empty-catalog handling (you need this regardless).
- **WooCommerce baseline per tenant** — ensuring Shop/Cart/Checkout/My Account pages exist and core options are set (logic stays the same; only occasional tweaks if you change checkout flow).
- **Provisioning hardening** — passwords, URLs, Tier A “don’t clobber merchant customization” rules for `tenant/config`.

**Do after theme look & feel is settled (or at least after IA is locked)**

- **Primary menu structure** — item labels, order, what links to pages vs Shop vs custom URLs depend on how you want the header to read.
- **Starter pages + homepage** — real copy, block patterns, hero sections, and “reading” settings should match the theme’s templates and patterns; redoing this twice is expensive.
- **Category/collection story** — if the theme’s shop/archive UX assumes certain taxonomies or landing pages, seed those once the theme defines them.

**Pragmatic compromise**

- Freeze **information architecture** first (which pages exist, one vs many menus, static front page or not)—doesn’t require final polish.
- Then ship **minimal seeding** (WC pages + one sparse menu + placeholders) only if you need demos/testing; treat it as **throwaway** until theme sign‑off.

**Short answer:** Finish **theme look & feel (and IA)** enough that menu locations, homepage template, and key patterns won’t churn—then invest in “looks manually built” seeding. Until then, focus on **sync + Woo baseline + provisioning**, which you’ll keep either way.

[REDACTED]

ok let's start working on the PLP. Remove the "shop" title component and remove the empty spacing from the top of the page where you're removing the title of the page
</user_query>

content.text:
Removing the PLP title via WooCommerce's `woocommerce_show_page_title` filter and tightening Storefront's `.woocommerce-products-header` and breadcrumb spacing on archive templates.

[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

Here’s what was implemented for the **PLP** (main shop + product taxonomies):

### 1. [`printonet-theme/functions.php`](printonet-multitenant/wp-content/themes/printonet-theme/functions.php)
- Added a filter on **`woocommerce_show_page_title`** so on **`is_shop()`** and **`is_product_taxonomy()`** the default WooCommerce archive title (the “Shop” / category `<h1>` from [`loop/header.php`](https://github.com/woocommerce/woocommerce/blob/trunk/plugins/woocommerce/templates/loop/header.php)) is not output.

### 2. [`printonet-theme/style.css`](printonet-multitenant/wp-content/themes/printonet-theme/style.css)
- Reset **padding/margin** on **`.woocommerce-products-header`** for shop and product archives so Storefront’s full-width rule (`padding: 0 0 …` + centered title block) doesn’t leave a tall empty band after the title is gone.
- Tightened **`.storefront-breadcrumb`** on those templates (smaller top padding, controlled bottom margin) so the stack sits closer to the header.

**Note:** Archive **descriptions** (e.g. category intro copy or shop page content) still run inside `.woocommerce-products-header`; only the **title** is removed.

If you sometimes run **`printonet-storefront`** as the active theme instead of **`printonet-theme`**, say so—the same changes would need to be duplicated there.

content.text:
Adding a theme override for WooCommerce's `loop/header.php` (the filter can be overridden by plugins or fail if `$show` isn't a bool). Adding a CSS fallback to hide any remaining archive title markup.

[REDACTED]

input.contents:
<?php
/**
 * Product archive header — title omitted for PLP (Printonet).
 *
 * Archive descriptions (category copy, shop page intro) still render via
 * woocommerce_archive_description.
 *
 * @package WooCommerce\Templates
 * @version 8.6.0
 */

if (!defined('ABSPATH')) {
    exit;
}

?>
<header class="woocommerce-products-header woocommerce-products-header--no-title">
	<?php
	/**
	 * Hook: woocommerce_archive_description.
	 *
	 * @hooked woocommerce_taxonomy_archive_description - 10
	 * @hooked woocommerce_product_archive_description - 10
	 */
	do_action('woocommerce_archive_description');
	?>
</header>

content.text:
Removing the `remove_action` — it prevents `woocommerce_product_taxonomy_archive_header()` from running, which loads `loop/header.php` (including our override). Relying on the template override and filter only.

[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

Here’s what was going wrong and what we changed.

### Why the filter wasn’t enough
`woocommerce_show_page_title` only affects the `<h1>` inside WooCommerce’s **`loop/header.php`**. Some setups still show a title because:

- Another callback runs **later** and changes the flag, or  
- The heading isn’t that markup (e.g. **`.page-header .page-title`** from a generic archive layout), or  
- Template overrides / caching meant the child theme wasn’t actually loading that partial.

### What we did

1. **Theme override** — Added [`printonet-theme/woocommerce/loop/header.php`](printonet-multitenant/wp-content/themes/printonet-theme/woocommerce/loop/header.php) so the PLP header **never prints the H1** but still runs **`woocommerce_archive_description`** (category / shop intro text).

2. **Stronger filter** — Left `woocommerce_show_page_title` → `false` on shop / product archives at **priority 999** so it wins over other plugins.

3. **CSS fallback** — In [`style.css`](printonet-multitenant/wp-content/themes/printonet-theme/style.css), hide `.woocommerce-products-header__title.page-title` and **`.page-header .page-title`** on shop so stray markup doesn’t show.

After deploy, if you still see the old header, clear WooCommerce’s template cache when available (**WooCommerce → Status → Tools → “Clear template cache”**) or bump the theme so caches invalidate. Hard-refresh the browser as well.

input.description: Read blockified archive-product WooCommerce template

content.text:
Identifying the issue: WooCommerce's blockified Product Catalog template uses `core/query-title`, which bypasses PHP `loop/header.php`. Removing that block's output on shop/product archives.

[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

Here’s what was actually going wrong:

### Root cause
WooCommerce’s **blockified Product Catalog** template (`templates/templates/blockified/archive-product.html`) outputs:

`<!-- wp:query-title {"type":"archive",...} /-->`

That **`core/query-title`** block prints the big **“Shop”** (and similar archive titles). It does **not** use `loop/header.php`, `woocommerce_show_page_title`, or your CSS tied only to `.woocommerce-products-header`, so **none of the earlier changes could affect it**.

### Fix (in theme)
1. **`render_block` filter** in [`functions.php`](printonet-multitenant/wp-content/themes/printonet-theme/functions.php): for **`core/query-title`** with **`type === archive`**, on **`is_shop()`** or **`is_product_taxonomy()`**, return an empty string so that heading is not rendered (blog/post archives are untouched).

2. **CSS backup** in [`style.css`](printonet-multitenant/wp-content/themes/printonet-theme/style.css): hide **`.wp-block-query-title`** on those same PLP body classes.

Classic PHP archives are still covered by your existing `loop/header.php` + filters.

### What you need to do
1. **Deploy** the updated `printonet-theme` to the server (this only helps if the live site is loading these files).
2. Hard-refresh or purge caches.

If it still doesn’t move after deploy, the active theme on that subsite isn’t `printonet-theme`—worth confirming under **Appearance → Themes** for that store.

[REDACTED]
